what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard ledger item that records an assessment of the 'iOS app + Sync' feature: it describes the user value of mobile Obsidian access, identifies a concrete infrastructure gate (the files live in WSL and Obsidian mobile sync requires a Windows-side vault), and warns that enabling mobile access would require a mirroring/bridging step with source-of-truth and conflict implications.
observations: Title frames the item as high value to Tim and high friction, gated on infrastructure.
Body is numbered section 6 of a larger list (P9).
Files are stored at /home/tim/company/channel-memory/noticeboard/ inside WSL.
WSL paths do not receive Obsidian mobile sync; the synced vault is Windows-side.
Mobile Obsidian over these files requires moving or mirroring them into a Windows-side, Sync-enabled vault folder.
That bridging step reopens source-of-truth and conflict questions across a filesystem boundary.
Frontmatter includes id, address, type block, source claude_code, state current, author_session, channel dragnet-development, and a part_of link to board://item-3480c157.
Created and updated timestamps are identical, indicating an initial filing.
standouts: Explicit 'highest value / highest friction' framing in the title.
Concrete path-based blocker rather than vague technical debt.
Warning that the feature is an infrastructure project, not a toggle.
Source-of-truth and conflict risk introduced by crossing the WSL/Windows boundary.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item frontmatter schema with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, thread, links, timestamps, and history.
Uses a markdown body with a numbered heading and bold emphasis.
References an external memory item ('canonical-vault-path') by name.
Uses a part_of link to relate this item to a parent item.
concerns: Relies on unstated context: who Tim is, what 'everything from my phone' means, and what the canonical-vault-path memory says.
No explicit acceptance criteria, next step, or proposed mirroring design.
Strong value judgment in the title may bias readers before they read the caveats.
notes: A mobile-sync feature assessment whose main takeaway is that the value is large but the path is blocked by WSL/Windows vault location, making it an infrastructure project first.
questions: What is P9 and what are the other numbered items?
What is item-3480c157 and how does this item fit into it?
What exactly does the canonical-vault-path memory state?
Who is Tim and what does 'everything from my phone' refer to?
Is there already a Windows-side Obsidian vault, or would one need to be created?
What mirroring/bridging approach is preferred (git, rsync, Obsidian sync folder, etc.)?
What are the acceptance criteria and next steps for this item?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to assess the iOS app + Sync feature and its feasibility; the file does exactly that, so they match.
intention_signals: The item seems to be trying to advocate for mobile Obsidian access while simultaneously protecting the project from treating it as a quick win by surfacing the WSL/Windows sync boundary as a real, prerequisite infrastructure problem.
novelty: It reframes a high-value user-facing capability as an infrastructure project because the source files reside in WSL, which is incompatible with Obsidian's mobile sync model.
contribution: This file contributes a concrete decision constraint (WSL path blocks mobile sync), a value/friction framing for iOS + Sync, and a warning about source-of-truth/conflict risk that a fused system would keep when planning mobile access.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard item assessing the iOS app + Sync feature for an Obsidian vault, noting high user value but a hard infrastructure gate because the vault lives in WSL while mobile sync requires a Windows-side folder.
intention_for_embedding: This item was filed to capture why mobile Obsidian access is valuable yet blocked by the WSL/Windows filesystem boundary and to prevent it from being treated as a simple toggle.
